title: Module-level singleton pool has no mutex: concurrent callers can race to create/swap the pool
description: The module-level `pool` and `poolFactory` variables in `getOrCreatePool` are read and mutated without any lock. Two concurrent `decodeTiledViewportPooled` calls with the same factory can both see `pool === null`, both call `new PyramidWorkerPool(...)`, then both assign to the singleton â€” one assignment is silently dropped, leaking the discarded pool (its workers are never terminated). Even the factory-swap check (`pool.activeCount > 0`) is a TOCTOU: activeCount can change between the read and the destroy call that follows.

title: prewarmAsync state transition races: concurrent calls can push state back to Prewarming after Active
description: `prewarmAsync` unconditionally sets `this.state = PoolState.Prewarming` at line 348 and then `this.state = PoolState.Active` on completion. If `prewarm()` (fire-and-forget) is called while a prior `prewarmAsync` is still in flight (e.g. from the visibility listener racing with the constructor), the second call drives state back to `Prewarming`, then both settle to `Active` independently â€” correct in practice because JS is single-threaded, but `this.state` transitions can interleave in unexpected order across multiple `await` boundaries, potentially confusing the `acquire()` guard that checks `PoolState.Draining/Destroyed`.

title: buffersInFlight check-then-add is not atomic: two concurrent decodes with the same outBuffer can both pass the guard
description: In `decodeTiledViewport`, the code checks `buffersInFlight.has(outBuf)` at line 151 and then calls `buffersInFlight.add(outBuf)` at line 156, with no synchronisation between the two. If two async decode calls referencing the same `outBuf` are both awaited concurrently (e.g. `Promise.all([decode(src, r1, {outBuffer: buf}), decode(src, r2, {outBuffer: buf})])`), both can complete the `has` check before either does the `add`, allowing both to proceed â€” producing silent pixel corruption from concurrent writes into the same buffer. The same pattern exists in `decodeWhole` (lines 550-556) and in `decodeTiledViewportPooled` (lines 1142-1153).

title: destroy() leaks active-drain interval on exception and uses polling instead of a Promise-based drain
description: `destroy()` installs a `setInterval` to wait for `this.active.size === 0`, then races it against a grace-period timeout. The interval is cleared inside the resolution callback, but if `Promise.race` itself throws (e.g. if the interval or the timeout Promise reject unexpectedly), `clearInterval` is never reached â€” leaking the timer indefinitely. Additionally, the 10 ms polling interval is wasteful; a resolve-callback registered when each `release()` zeros `active.size` would be cleaner and have zero residual timers.

title: decodeTilesParallel coroutine loop checks failed/aborted after awaiting the next tile, not before claiming it
description: Each coroutine in `decodeTilesParallel` reads `const idx = next++` unconditionally (line 951), then checks `if (failed || controller.signal.aborted) break` at the top of the next iteration. This means a tile index is always consumed before the abort/failure condition is visible â€” one extra tile per coroutine is claimed (incrementing `next`) but never decoded after a failure, which is benign for correctness but wastes one slot per live coroutine and can confuse progress telemetry (completedCount undershoots total). The `signal.aborted` check should precede the `next++`.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled does not release acquired handles on the early-exit direct-decode paths
description: After `p.acquire(desired)` succeeds (line 1250), several early-exit paths exist (lines 1252-1263): if `liveHandles.length === 0` the code falls through to a direct decode and returns without calling `p.release`. If `usable.length === 0` (all handles bad), the code calls `p.release(liveHandles)` correctly. But the first branch (empty handles) skips both the `try { ... } finally { p.release(liveHandles) }` block AND the outer `finally { buffersInFlight.delete }`. On second inspection, the empty-handles path precedes the `try/finally`, so the `buffersInFlight` deletion at line 1383 is also bypassed â€” leaving the caller's `outBuffer` permanently in `buffersInFlight`.

title: ensureIccProfile assigns a Promise to source._iccProfile without guarding concurrent first-call callers
description: `ensureIccProfile` checks `if (key in source)` then assigns a Promise to `source[key]`. In a single-threaded JS event loop this works â€” the check and assign are synchronous before the first `await`. However, if two callers run `ensureIccProfile` on the same source object before the first one has stored the Promise (i.e., both call before any microtask yields), both will see `!(key in source)` and both will create independent decoder instances, wasting WASM heap. More critically, the second allocation races the first decoder's `dispose()` call. The fix is to assign the Promise synchronously before the first `await`, which the code does, so this is low probability â€” but the description is accurate for the window between the `in` check and the assignment on the next line when called from two non-async contexts.

title: Module-level idCounter and WeakMaps in cache.ts are not safe if multiple realm instances share the same module
description: The `idCounter`, `levelIdBySource`, and `bufIdByBuffer` WeakMaps are module-level singletons. The doc comment on `PyramidCache` correctly warns that "Cache IDs are realm-local: if tiles decode in workers the cache must live on exactly one thread (main)." However, nothing enforces this â€” if `cache.ts` is accidentally imported in a worker (e.g. via a shared bundle), a second `idCounter` starts at 0 and generates IDs that collide with main-thread IDs. The existing comment is the only guard; a runtime assertion or explicit realm check would make violations fail fast.

title: decodeTilesParallel creates an internal AbortController but does not abort it when the outer signal fires before the for-loop starts
description: If `opts.signal` is already aborted when `decodeTilesParallel` is called, the function correctly throws at line 903-905. But if `opts.signal` is aborted between the `aborted` check and the `outerAbortListener` registration (lines 914-918), the bridge listener is registered but the internal `controller` is never aborted â€” so per-tile workers see `effectiveSignal` as non-aborted for the duration of the batch. The window is zero in single-threaded JS (no preemption), but the pre-check at line 916 (`if (opts.signal.aborted) controller.abort()`) only runs once at registration, not on re-check after the `await` at line 1031.

title: Waiter queue in acquire() uses a plain setTimeout for expiry but does not cancel the timeout on early resolution
description: When `acquire()` enqueues a waiter (lines 514-529), it arms a `setTimeout` for `maxWait`. If the waiter is resolved early by a `release()` call (line 573-574), the timeout fires anyway â€” it removes itself from `waiters` (already gone) and calls `resolve(got)` on an already-settled Promise, which is silently ignored. This is a minor unnecessary timer leak (one timeout per over-cap acquire), not a correctness issue. Storing the timer reference in the waiter object and clearing it on early resolution would eliminate the ghost timer.

category: missing abort propagation on pooled path
title: Fallback direct-decode paths inside decodeTiledViewportPooled do not check signal before starting decode
description: Multiple fallback return sites after `p.acquire` use `signal ? await raceWithAbort(...) : await decodeRegion(...)`. The `raceWithAbort` correctly handles an already-aborted signal. However the path where `liveHandles.length === 0` (line 1252) and the path where all handles are bad (line 1261) both go through `raceWithAbort` fine. The concern is that the outer `finally` at line 1382 that deletes `outBuffer` from `buffersInFlight` is not reached from these paths (already flagged as concurrency-6). If that is fixed, signal propagation is already correct.
